1: 'The apostles and the believers throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God' (ton logon tou theou — the word of God; disp=3 for logos) — the 'word of God' as received-message is the standard Acts phrase for the gospel proclamation and its reception (Acts 6:7; 8:14; 13:5, 7, 44, 46, 48; 17:13). The news travels quickly: the entire Judean church hears about the Gentile reception. The 'also' (kai ta ethnē — even the Gentiles) captures the surprise: the word has crossed the ethnic boundary.
2: 'The circumcised believers (hoi ek peritomēs — those of the circumcision) took issue with him' — oi ek peritomēs (those from circumcision) is the technical designation for Jewish believers who maintained circumcision as a boundary-marker; they appear again in Gal 2:12. 'Took issue with' (diekrinonto — were discriminating against/disputing with) uses the same root as diakrinō in 10:20 (do not discriminate/hesitate): the very people who make ethnic-distinctions dispute with Peter for not making them.
3: 'You went into the house of uncircumcised men and ate with them' — the charge focuses on table-fellowship rather than the baptism. In purity-law terms, entering the home of the uncircumcised and sharing a meal was the greater social/ritual transgression. Peter's defense (vv.4-17) will recount the vision, the Spirit's direct instruction, the Spirit's falling on Cornelius's household before baptism, and the disciples' testimony — a fourfold defense for a single act of table-fellowship.
4: 'Starting from the beginning, Peter explained everything to them in order (kathexēs exetitheto autois — he was setting it out in order for them)' — exetitheto (he was explaining/setting out, imperfect = continuous explanation) and kathexēs (in order, systematically — Luke's characteristic word for sequential narrative, Luke 1:3; Acts 18:23) describe a methodical defense. Peter does not abbreviate; he goes from the beginning, ensuring every element of the divine initiation is presented before the conclusion is drawn.
5: 'I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision' — Peter's first-person retelling repeats the key elements of the 10:9-16 account but from the perspective of testimony rather than narration. 'In a trance' (en ekstasei — in an ecstasy/standing-outside-oneself) — the same ekstasis as 10:10. Peter's identification of the visionary state is part of his defense: this was not a dream or an ordinary perception but a Spirit-given vision.
6: 'I looked into it and saw four-footed animals of the earth, wild beasts, reptiles and birds' — the expansion of the animal-list (tēs gēs ta tetrapoda kai ta thēria kai ta herpeta kai ta peteina tou ouranou — adding thēria, wild beasts) compared to the original account (10:12 — no thēria listed) may reflect a different tradition, or Peter's own recollection adding detail. Either way, the point is the same: all animals, clean and unclean together, presented without category-distinction.
7: 'I also heard a voice telling me, Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' — Peter's retelling uses the same command-words as 10:13 (Anastas, Petre, thuson kai phage) verbatim. The repetition of the divine command in testimony is the standard confirmatory structure: the same words God said, repeated exactly, constitute the divine evidence for what followed. The unchanged divine word is the unchanging divine will.
8: 'I replied, Surely not, Lord! Nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth' — Peter's protest is retold with a nuance: 10:14 said 'I have never eaten anything impure'; 11:8 says 'nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth' (eis to stoma mou — into my mouth). The 'into my mouth' addition may reflect the purity-law's emphasis on the oral reception of impurity (Lev 11 concludes with food entering the body). Peter's reformulation of his original refusal strengthens the case for what followed: his objection was total; God's response was decisive.
9: 'The voice spoke from heaven a second time, Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' — the heavenly voice (phōnē ek tou ouranou — voice from heaven, the bath qol equivalent) repeating the command emphasizes divine authority. 'A second time' (ek deuterou — from the second) — the full three repetitions of 10:15-16 are compressed here to the essential theological statement. What God has cleansed (ekatharistern — perfect tense: completed, permanent cleansing) must not be called common by any human authority.
10: 'This happened three times, and then it was all pulled back up to heaven' — the threefold repetition and withdrawal are stated concisely in testimony. Peter presents the triple-confirmation as the divine insistence that overrode his initial refusal. The withdrawal-to-heaven (anespasthē panta eis ton ouranon) finalizes the vision's message: what came from heaven has returned to heaven, having delivered its meaning.
11: 'Right then three men who had been sent to me from Caesarea stopped at the house where I was staying' — the 'right then' (kai idou — and behold!) is Luke's particle of providential coincidence: the three-men's arrival precisely as the vision ends is the divine punctuation that interprets the vision. The Cornelius-connection (men from Caesarea) becomes the vision's interpretation: the sheet's animals = the uncircumcised Cornelius and his household.
12: 'The Spirit told me to have no hesitation about going with them (mēden diakrinanta — discriminating nothing)' — the participial mēden diakrinanta (making no distinction) extends and clarifies the 10:20 command: the Spirit explicitly named the ethnic-distinction impulse as what must be overridden. 'These six brothers also went with me, and we entered the man's house' — the six witnesses (hexakis adelphoi — six brothers; Acts 10:23 says 'some of the believers from Joppa') provide the Deuteronomic witness-number for the defense.
13: 'He told us how he had seen an angel appear in his house and say, Send to Joppa for Simon who is called Peter' — the angel's instruction to Cornelius, reported by Cornelius, reported now by Peter to Jerusalem, constitutes a triple-verified divine initiation: the angel told Cornelius, Cornelius told the messengers, the messengers told Peter, Peter now tells Jerusalem. Every link in the chain independently confirms the divine origin of the Cornelius encounter.
14: 'He will bring you a message (rhēmata — words/things said) through which you and all your household will be saved' — rhēmata (specific spoken words, things said) rather than logos (word/message generally) — the angel specifies the form of salvation: through words spoken by Peter. The 'and all your household' anticipates the household-salvation pattern: Cornelius's salvation extends to the entire social unit under his headship.
15: 'As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit came on them as he had come on us at the beginning' — 'as I began to speak' (en tō arxasthai me lalein — in my beginning to speak) emphasizes the Spirit's interruption of the sermon: before Peter finished, the Spirit fell. The en archē (at the beginning) parallel — 'as he had come on us at the beginning' — identifies the Cornelius event as a second Pentecost. Pneuma hagiou (Holy Spirit, disp=3 and disp=2 for hagios) — both terms capitalized in the MKT.
16: 'Then I remembered what the Lord had said: John baptised with water, but you will be baptised with the Holy Spirit' — the verb emnēsthēn (I remembered) marks the moment of pneumatological recognition: Peter interprets what he is witnessing through Jesus's words. The Lord's prophetic word (rhēmatos — specific spoken word) becomes the interpretive key for understanding the Spirit's falling on Gentiles. Both 'Holy Spirit' instances (pneumatos hagiou, disp=3 and 2) are capitalized in the MKT.
17: 'So if God gave them the same gift he gave us, who believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?' — the 'same gift' (tēn isēn dōrean — the equal/identical gift) asserts the qualitative equivalence of the Cornelius Pentecost with the Acts 2 Pentecost. 'Who was I to think that I could oppose God' (egō tis ēmēn dynatos kōlysai ton theon — I who was I able to prevent God?) — kōlyō (prevent) echoes the baptism-prevention question of 10:47: no one can withhold baptism from those whom God has already baptised with the Spirit.
18: 'When they heard this, they had no further objections and praised God, saying, So then, even to Gentiles God has granted repentance that leads to life' — hēsychasan (they became quiet, ceased objecting) is the silence of conviction before the evidence. 'Life' (zōēn — life, disp=2) in 'repentance unto life' (metanoian eis zōēn) is eschatological life — the same zōē used for eternal life throughout John's Gospel. The Jerusalem church's doxology names the theological conclusion: God has granted the Gentiles repentance (the gift of being able to turn) and life (the result of turning).
19: 'Those who had been scattered by the persecution that broke out when Stephen was killed traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus and Antioch, spreading the word (ton logon — the word; disp=3) only among Jews' — the Stephen-persecution scattering (8:1 — 'they were all scattered') continues to produce mission. The scattered ones travel the Mediterranean coastal route. 'Spreading the word only among Jews' (mēdeni lalountes ton logon ei mē monon Ioudaiois — speaking the word to no one except Jews only) — the ethnic restriction of the initial mission is the baseline from which the next verse departs.
20: 'However, some of them... began to speak to Greeks also, proclaiming the good news about the Lord Jesus' — the 'however' (de — but) introduces the innovative deviation: some men from Cyprus and Cyrene spoke to Hellenistai (Greek-speakers, likely Gentile Greeks rather than Greek-speaking Jews). The transition from Jewish-only to Greek-inclusive proclamation happens not through a Jerusalem decision but through unnamed Cypriot and Cyrenian missionaries following the Spirit's impulse. The Cornelius episode has cleared the theological air; these missionaries breathe it.
21: 'The Lord's hand was with them, and a great number of people believed and turned to the Lord' — 'the Lord's hand' (cheir Kyriou — the hand of the Lord) is the OT power-formula for divine intervention (Exod 9:3; 1 Sam 5:6; Isa 59:1; Ezek 1:3). Luke uses the OT power-language for the Antioch mission: the same divine hand that accomplished the exodus and the prophets' ministry now accompanies the Gentile-inclusive gospel-mission. Epistrepsas (turned/turned back) — the conversion-verb used for Israel's return to YHWH in the prophets (Hos 6:1; Zech 1:3-4).
22: 'News of this reached the church at Jerusalem (ēkousthē ho logos — the word/report was heard) and they sent Barnabas to Antioch' — logos (disp=3) here is the 'report/news' sense, not the gospel-proclamation sense: the Jerusalem church heard the news-word about Antioch. They sent Barnabas (the 'Son of Encouragement,' 4:36) — the choice of Barnabas, himself a Cypriot diaspora Jew (4:36), to investigate the Cypriot-Cyrenian mission to Gentile Greeks is providentially apt.
23: 'When he arrived and saw what the grace of God had done (tēn charin tēn tou theou — the grace of God), he was glad and encouraged them all to remain true to the Lord with all their hearts' — the 'grace of God' is both what Barnabas observes and what he names: he sees the changed lives and calls them grace. 'With all their hearts' (tē prothesei tēs kardias — the purpose/intention of the heart) — prothesis (purpose, resolve, intention) indicates deliberate commitment rather than emotional response: the kind of covenant-faithfulness that the heart-circumcision language of Acts 7:51 points toward.
24: 'He was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a great number of people were brought to the Lord' — Luke's character-summary of Barnabas (agapethos anthropos — a good man) uses the same vocabulary applied to Cornelius (agathos kai dikaios — good and righteous in secular Greek, though not exactly in this text). 'Full of the Holy Spirit and faith' — the double fullness (Spirit and faith) is the apostolic qualification formula (Stephen 6:5; 7:55). The great-number result (prosetethe ochlos hikanos — a sufficient crowd was added) is the standard Lukan response to Spirit-filled ministry.
25: 'Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul' — Barnabas's initiative in searching for Saul (anazētein Saulon — to search up/out Saul) recalls that Barnabas was the one who first vouched for the converted Saul to the Jerusalem church (9:27). Barnabas recognizes that Antioch's Gentile-inclusive mission needs Paul's particular gifting: the apostle to the Gentiles (Gal 1:16; Rom 11:13) for the church that is learning to be Gentile-inclusive. The Spirit-directed appointment of the right person for the right mission is a Lukan pattern.
26: 'And when he found him, he brought him to Antioch. So for a whole year Barnabas and Saul met with the church and taught great numbers of people' — the year-long intensive teaching ministry (eniautos holon — an entire year) in Antioch is the first concentrated church-planting effort in a predominantly Gentile context. 'The disciples were called Christians (Christianous — ones of Christ) first at Antioch' — Christianos (a Latin-style formation: one belonging to Christus) is either a name given by outsiders or adopted by the community. The suffix -ianos (-ian) is used for the partisans of a political leader or household; 'Christians' = those of the household of Christ.
27: 'During this time some prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch' — the movement of prophets (prophētai) from Jerusalem to Antioch is the inverse of the typical pilgrimage direction: Jerusalem's spiritual resources flow toward the new Gentile center. Acts consistently shows the charisms of the Jerusalem community (prophets, apostles) traveling to confirm and strengthen the mission churches that the Spirit is planting.
28: 'Agabus stood up and through the Spirit predicted (esēmanen dia tou pneumatos — he indicated/signified through the Spirit) that a severe famine would spread over the entire Roman world' — sēmainō (to signify, indicate) is John's vocabulary for prophetic sign-speech (John 12:33; 18:32; 21:19; Rev 1:1). Agabus's prophecy is a sēmeion-speech: not merely information but a divine sign-communication. 'Through the Spirit' (dia tou pneumatos) is the authentic-prophetic formula — his word comes through the Spirit's mediation, not his own insight.
29: 'The disciples, as each one was able, decided to provide help for the brothers and sisters living in Judea' — kathōs euporeito tis (as each one prospered/was able) is the voluntary, ability-proportioned giving that the Corinthians will be instructed in (1 Cor 16:2 — 'each of you should put aside a sum in keeping with his income'). The Antioch collection for Judean famine-relief is the first instance of what will become the characteristic inter-church collection Paul organizes throughout his ministry (1 Cor 16:1-4; 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28).
30: 'This they did, sending their gift to the elders by Barnabas and Saul' — the 'elders' (presbyterous — the same term used for the Ephesian elders in 20:17) receive the collection for distribution. This is the first mention of 'elders' as a leadership structure in the Jerusalem church; the term brings Jewish synagogue-governance into the church's administrative vocabulary. Barnabas and Saul carry the collection: the relief mission to Jerusalem will end with Paul's arrest (21:27-36), making this first Jerusalem visit a foretaste of Paul's final Jerusalem visit.
